speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. Survivor Pola Grinstein. October 23, 1996. Mrs. Grinstein, how far were you from your hometown all this time that you were going around hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20 kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, what I say? No, less, less, about 10 kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you  ever have a chance to go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? It was right away when I went-- I-- I came to Olecko. The-- the [INAUDIBLE], the Goy, which I was hiding there, you wanted-- I'll tell you how I was liberated. I was liberated by [INAUDIBLE]. And when I saw the soldiers, they are Russian. But we were hiding. Then, one saw us hiding, me and Sanja. Then, they told-- Sanja what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sanja Feldman. He say, what is the two-- two girls are doing here? He say, this is my sister's girls. They are coming here, because the Polish burning their village. He said, no, this is not your sisters. You tell me better the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a Russian soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Russian. Then, he was afraid. He say, OK. You must to-- to tell. Then, I-- I-- I told the lady-- he-- he came to me. This was [INAUDIBLE]. He say, look, I want to know. Because now is war. And you must, because children are the-- the best spies. I say, no. I cannot tell you here. Then, you'll tell-- he said, what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The [INAUDIBLE] used to shoot the Russian Army. I say, no. If you'll take me in a-- in a separate room, I'll tell you. He took me in separate room and said, who you are? I say, I am a Jewish girl. I was here hiding. I start to cry like a baby. And he said, my God, I don't believe in God, but I was sending-- you've been through so much. This-- no-- Suffering.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suffering. And I needed to speak so-- to you. And you know, and he came out and he told-- I know that I said, but I said, don't tell nobody because you'll go and they'll burn all the family. There was a family about 12 people. Then he-- he-- they say, no, no, no. He say, I still need to-- some questions to ask. And then-- and then they took him-- the Goy-- to-- to burn the-- the-- the-- the-- the dead people. And then he saw our Jewish-- he didn't know what to do with us. The-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were guarding almost. They were guarding. And he knew he was afraid they'll kill us. Then, he-- he-- he went to-- to the-- to work, and there, he saw this officer-- a Jewish-- a Jewish nose. He said, you are Jewish? He say, yes. You were crying to so-and-so. You want maybe to kill me too? He say, no, no, no. I have two girls which I hided. And on that morning, somebody saw us. And I say, Sanja, I don't know. I-- I shouldn't go. I shouldn't go. We should be here. I don't know why we should be here. And he said, look, I'll go with you but in case, if they are not there, I'll kill you. He took two soldiers with him. I'll kill you with whole family, because you are liar. He came, you know, and it was good that we didn't go. If we would go, he would kill the whole family. And he say-- he say, go out, people. Go out. And he start to-- he was from Kiev. He start to cry. And he told [INAUDIBLE]. And he gave him the two good horses, and he took the bad for the soldiers. And he said, no more. He gave him no more to the-- to the war, to the front. And he say, now, you-- you need to do something. Go right away and take them to-- right away and take them to-- to-- to our city. He took us to Olecko.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Olecko?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Olecko, where my sister and brother. This was the nearest. He took to Olecko. They-- they saw they were crying. Oh, my god. You brought, [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, no, no. I don't leave us-- you here. Then he brought us to the-- to the road of Rowne. It was-- Rowne was big bombardment, big-- the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they say the-- the-- the-- the soldiers, when they go, where are you going? They bomb-- they bombadier the Rowne. Where are you going? I said, we go on the market. And what we'll tell? We were afraid. When we came to Rowne, and there, we were liberated. And we were in Rowne all year, all year from-- When did you find your mother and brother again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the brother was killed. My brother my brother went to one, which by-- by a forest. He was a-- a-- a shoe repairman, a old one. And the poor brother, what do we say? You know [NON-ENGLISH]? You want to-- to-- to help me to repair shoes, then you can be. He say, OK. He was very OK. He never-- in our family was no shoes-- shoemaker, nothing. And he start and he say--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was Elia at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elia was 27, born '27.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was born in '27.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're talking '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: '42 or '43, yes. He was a 14-year-old, you know? And-- and-- and then he-- he say, you know the [INAUDIBLE], we could make a pair of shoes. We say, OK. Then he took, you know, boat leather and he make a pair of shoes. He saw how they're made, you know? He made a pair of shoes. And then he-- he-- he steal for us, too-- to-- leather and he made for us. We came barefoot in snow [INAUDIBLE]. He gave me and my-- me and Sanja, two pair of shoes he brought to us, We were in contact that time. And I say-- I say-- relief-- relief-- This is all-- I say I'm afraid that, you know, that I am there already. And then-- then they find out who he is, and they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: From [INAUDIBLE] farm was. They recognize him and they say, oh, this is David's son. You know who he is? You know? And there's-- there, oy, I don't want to know. And my mother, they told her all the things. She got distraught, and I took care a year, all year of her. She died in Rowne. I buried her.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, I forgot this in Rowne. From the--

speaker: interviewer
text: From what?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we went to, you know, to find-- to to Israel, the place where they-- where they kill the Jews. Then we find the shoes, these diapers, all kind of clothes. This was in-- in '44. This was like a year or two years of still [INAUDIBLE] we find. And when we-- when we-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you stepped on the grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we start to [INAUDIBLE]. When we start to [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Dig. to dig.

speaker: interviewee
text: To dig-- to dig around the-- the-- the grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We do that. We wanted to-- to know that this and this. We wanted to-- to put on this stone, but we didn't have time. We left in '45. In '45, we left already Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left Rowne. But in Rowne, you were digging in the graves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, digging around the grave. I don't remember, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I don't remember the name of the-- of the-- was a small-- a small, not a forest, how you say, a [INAUDIBLE]. A park. A park.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went and you found--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went and we found all kind of [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some bones also buried there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever tell you where your father was buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: I they told me, but I couldn't go there. I-- as a pauper, after the war-- after the war, I took such revenge of this what killed my father, the ones that took away everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? They-- I came to my place and there was the Czech was still there. And I came to the-- to the mayor, you know, and I recognized all the-- the equipment and everything. I said, oy, this is mine. He said, oh, no this is not yours. Go right away from here. And you know, I-- I took very bad steps. He could kill me with Sanja, and we ran away. And I came to the neighbor, a Czech, first, which they neighbored our--

speaker: interviewer
text: Farms.

speaker: interviewee
text: Farms, yes. And I called-- told her and I-- I cried very much, and she gave me bread and-- and this--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was kind to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bacon-- and bacon. And at night, she was very rich, a nice house-- came the- the-- not [INAUDIBLE], the-- the like-- the-- the-- from the-- from the Army, like in come from the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they made there their-- their headquarter there. And the soldiers were in the-- in the forest. There was a big forest where our village was. And she told the whole story. They went to him. They arrested him. They arrested him, and they brought him in the shtetl and I was there, yes. And-- and-- and he says come in to Lutsk, you must be a witness and I was a witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told, I think [INAUDIBLE], I told that he took away everything and he-- he almost killed me, which was-- was-- was true. And he got death penalty, and then he-- he-- he told who killed, yes. He told who killed, you know? He gave out everyone who killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I came to my shtetl. They told me that Kowalski, the commandant from the shtetl, the Ukrainian, which, you know, organized to rape and to-- and to kill and to take away, he was there. They-- they caught him and I was another witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a witness for that trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: For that trial and some others, I was, I was. I did my-- my job. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: From-- from Rowne, where-- you ended up in a DP camp you told me before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, on there-- in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Munich? How long did you stay in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Munich from '45 to '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go by airplane.

speaker: interviewer
text: By airplane?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did anyone contact you or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I found my brother's brother-in-law in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he sponsored you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no need to sponsor right away. I had-- I was nurse in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: A nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I finished nursing school in Germany. And there was a [INAUDIBLE] from Israel, a [INAUDIBLE] which know only Hebrew and he broke a leg, and nobody could understand him. And they had-- they know that I speak Hebrew. They took me and I translated. And he say, what? We saw Hebrew, you want to go to America? I'll send you [INAUDIBLE]. What day you want? And I say-- and I say, OK. And he influenced on me that I go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who paid for your studies in Munich as a-- to be a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no pay. The Joint helped us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, gave us packages and the German-- we had [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel, how old were you when you got to Israel? How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One moment. I'm so [INAUDIBLE]. '25, '49. A 24-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 24 years old. Did you meet someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I meet a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone special?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone special? I-- I meet-- I was engaged to a doctor, but didn't go. I changed my mind. And then I met my husband. He was-- he-- he was a-- a Russian. He finished MH degree-- MA degree-- and in Israel, he started a Ph degree and he finished in Los Angeles with a Ph degree in UCLA.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michel Grinstein. He had a father her. He was hiding with a father. Also also by a-- by a Baptist. 12 people-- Pavloski-- 12 people he-- he was hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry? What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In In Israel. Did you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I miscarriaged after and before, and I had one son.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David. Like my father. And he was sick and he had cancer on the thyroid. And he's buried here in Florida. I was in Los Angeles. We used to live in Los Angeles. He went here to college.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he when--

speaker: interviewee
text: 23. He woul be 36. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go from Israel to Los Angeles, or to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Los Angeles I directly. He was sick and asthma. He had very bad asthma. And I was waiting, waiting, but I couldn't wait anymore because he was so small and he got-- got his [INAUDIBLE] And the second day, he start to be blue. And I decided to go, and we went here and right away, he was 6'2' He was tall and dark and handsome. And he went through from the asthma. And I think, Thank God. Thank God. And then this. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you move to Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Florida I moved in '83. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A woman with your experience and your wisdom, what kind of message would leave to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, not to-- not to trust people, not to trust the countries, not to trust, you know, leaders too much. To remember what happened by Hitler. It could be repeat again if we wouldn't be careful. Because in my time now, they say already there couldn't be. This a lie. In our time. Now, I want to say so. The Polacks, the Ukrainian, the Lithuanian, I don't know but they say, that the-- but the Ukraine and-- and the Polacks I went on my own skin. They are-- they are-- their-- their conscience is not clear because they are 99% is their fault that we suffered. We lost so much from our family. The-- if not there, would be in our-- in our section, from Ukraine would be alive today-- 60% people would be alive. Because the-- the German came three, four people and the rest was the-- the-- the-- the-- Banderas with the police and everything in the-- in the-- in the forest, where the people, the Jewish people ran out to the forest was killed through the [NON-ENGLISH] and Bandera, the party Bandera. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, they-- they deny. And most can write down, now, take sides that is not-- signatures that is not-- not true after the war kills it to take [INAUDIBLE], which went through by miracle alive to be 42 people to kill? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, you know, I feel shameful-- shameful, you know. This is also, he told me the story. He was alive, he, his son, and his father and a brother. There was three. You know, they came in the shtetl by-- by the-- and it was a small shtetl. And he went to see what-- with the brother what they can-- where to go. And the-- the son said [SPEAKING HEBREW] take me with you. Take me with you. He said, no, no. Be with the Zayde. He went with the Zayde. They come. They kill the Zayde, killed him. [INAUDIBLE]

words.text: and it was a small shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was-- where was that? Where did they kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in a shtetl in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the shtetl?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he's alive. He's here. He told me the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your testimony.
